name: Pridwen
description: Pridwen is the shield of King Arthur in Welsh mythology and Arthurian legend. While its exact properties vary across different texts, it is consistently portrayed as a protective and powerful artifact. In some accounts, Pridwen is described as bearing an image of the Virgin Mary, offering divine protection to its wielder. Other interpretations suggest it possesses the ability to transport Arthur and his warriors to the Otherworld or to render them invisible. The shield's significance lies in its representation of Arthur's strength, sovereignty, and divine favor. It symbolizes his ability to defend his kingdom and his people against both mortal and supernatural threats. The name 'Pridwen' itself is often interpreted to mean 'fair face' or 'white face', perhaps referring to its gleaming surface or the purity it represents. Its presence in various Arthurian tales underscores its importance as a symbol of Arthur's kingship and the battles he fought to maintain peace and justice in Britain. The shield is sometimes depicted as being large and round, capable of covering a warrior's entire body. Its construction is often attributed to skilled craftsmen of the mythical realm, imbuing it with magical properties beyond those of ordinary shields. Ultimately, Pridwen serves as a potent emblem of Arthur's chivalry, his commitment to his ideals, and his divinely ordained role as king.
subtitle: Arthur's Shield of Divine Protection
origin: Varies; sometimes divinely crafted or obtained through magical means
currentLocation: Unknown; lost to history and legend

keyMyths.title: The Spoils of Annwn
keyMyths.description: In the Welsh poem 'Preiddeu Annwfn' (The Spoils of Annwn), Arthur uses Pridwen to travel to the Otherworld and retrieve a magical cauldron. The shield's protective qualities are implied during this dangerous journey.

keyMyths.title: Battles of Arthur
keyMyths.description: While not always explicitly mentioned in battle descriptions, Pridwen is assumed to have been used by Arthur in his many campaigns to defend Britain. Its presence symbolizes his strength and divine right to rule.

sources.author: Geoffrey of Monmouth
sources.description: Early account of Arthur, though Pridwen is not prominently featured.
sources.text: Early account of Arthur, though Pridwen is not prominently featured.

sources.author: Welsh Tradition
sources.description: Welsh poem mentioning Pridwen's role in the raid on Annwn.
